name: Alto de Agiña and Aritxulegi Pass Loop
name_original: Collado de Aritxulegi y Alto Agiña — circular desde Irún
description: This difficult 33.1-mile road cycling route from Irún features challenging climbs, scenic views, and the Aritxulegi Tunnel.
region_intro: Waterfall hiking trails around Bajo Bidasoa traverse a diverse landscape in Gipuzkoa, Basque Country. The region is characterized by the mountainous terrain of Aiako Harria Natural Park, featuring ancient geological formations, dense forests of oak and beech, and numerous rivers. Mount Jaizkibel contributes varied terrain with steep slopes, cliffs, and ravines that feed into streams. The Bidasoa River itself flows through natural areas, creating wetlands and supporting a rich ecosystem. Best waterfall hiking trails around Bajo Bidasoa The most popular waterfall hiking route is Artikutza Village and Valley – Bianditz Summit loop from Endara, a 15.4 miles (24.8 km) trail that takes 8 hours 56 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers extensive views across the valley and towards the summit. Another top favourite among local hikers is Artikutza Village and Valley – Erroiarri Waterfall loop from Eskas, a difficult 10.0 miles (16.1 km) path. This trail leads through the Artikutza valley, featuring the Erroiarri Waterfall and varied forest scenery. Local hikers also love the Bianditz Summit – Artikutza Waterfall loop from Eskas, a 6.1 miles (9.8 km) trail leading through forested valleys and towards the Artikutza Waterfall, often completed in about 3 hours 39 minutes. Hiking around Bajo Bidasoa is defined by mountainous landscapes, dense forests, and river valleys, often featuring historical ruins. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to challenging ascents. The routes in Bajo Bidasoa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 1,400 reviews. More than 10,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Bajo Bidasoa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many waterfall hiking trails are there in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: Bajo Bidasoa features 7 dedicated waterfall hiking trails. These routes traverse the region's diverse landscapes, offering opportunities to explore cascades within the mountainous terrain and dense forests.

Q: Are there any easy waterfall hikes suitable for families?
A: While there are no trails specifically rated as 'easy' among the waterfall hikes, there are 2 moderate options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6977457" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bianditz Summit – Artikutza Waterfall loop from Eskas&lt;/a&gt; is a 9.8 km (6.1 miles) route with a moderate elevation gain, making it a good choice for families with some hiking experience. The region's diverse trails often include informational points and landmarks, adding to the family experience.

Q: What are the most challenging waterfall hikes in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: For those seeking a challenge, Bajo Bidasoa offers 5 difficult waterfall hiking trails. The most extensive is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33947644" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artikutza Village and Valley – Bianditz Summit loop from Endara&lt;/a&gt;, a 24.8 km (15.4 miles) route with over 1100 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3993035" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artikutza Village and Valley – Erroiarri Waterfall loop from Eskas&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 16.1 km (10.0 miles) and features the impressive Erroiarri Waterfall.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the waterfall hiking trails?
A: Many trails in the Bajo Bidasoa region are generally dog-friendly, especially those in natural parks like Aiako Harria. However, it's always recommended to check specific trail regulations or local signage, particularly within protected areas or private land, to ensure dogs are permitted and if they need to be on a leash. Always practice responsible pet ownership by cleaning up after your dog.

Q: Are there circular waterfall routes in the area?
A: Yes, several waterfall hikes in Bajo Bidasoa are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33947644" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artikutza Village and Valley – Bianditz Summit loop from Endara&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6977457" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bianditz Summit – Artikutza Waterfall loop from Eskas&lt;/a&gt;. These loops provide varied scenery without retracing your steps.

Q: What is the best time of year to visit the waterfalls in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: The best time to visit the waterfalls in Bajo Bidasoa is typically during spring (March to May) when snowmelt and seasonal rains ensure the cascades are at their most powerful and impressive. Autumn (September to November) can also be beautiful, with vibrant foliage and still-active waterfalls. Summers can see reduced water flow, while winters might offer a different, often quieter, experience, though some trails could be more challenging due to weather conditions.

Q: What can I see besides waterfalls on these hikes?
A: Beyond the captivating waterfalls, the trails in Bajo Bidasoa offer a rich tapestry of natural and historical sights. You can explore the ancient geological formations and dense forests of Aiako Harria Natural Park, or enjoy panoramic views from peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/295029" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaizkibel Summit&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/918649" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Irumugarrieta Peak (811 m)&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also pass by historical ruins, adding a cultural dimension to your outdoor adventure. Don't miss the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1841170" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aitzondo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, a notable natural monument in the region.

Q: Are there any hidden gem waterfall trails in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: While specific 'hidden gem' trails are often discovered through local exploration, the Artikutza valley, which features the Erroiarri Waterfall and the Artikutza Waterfall, offers a sense of secluded beauty. The region's dense forests and less-trafficked paths often lead to serene spots away from the main routes, providing a more intimate experience with nature. Exploring the various trails within Aiako Harria Natural Park can also reveal lesser-known cascades.

Q: Is wild swimming possible near any of the waterfalls?
A: While the region is rich in water features, including the Bidasoa River and numerous streams feeding the waterfalls, designated wild swimming spots near the cascades are not widely publicized. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4639053" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Erantzintxiki Natural Pool&lt;/a&gt; is a notable natural monument in the area that might offer opportunities for cooling off, but always exercise caution and check local regulations regarding swimming in natural bodies of water.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: The hiking trails in Bajo Bidasoa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 1,400 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's mountainous landscapes, dense forests, and river valleys, often highlighting the historical ruins encountered along the way. The variety of routes, from moderate paths to challenging ascents, is also a popular aspect, catering to different ability levels.

Q: Are there any permits required for waterfall hikes in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: Generally, permits are not required for hiking on public trails in Bajo Bidasoa, including those leading to waterfalls. However, some areas, particularly within natural parks or private estates like the Artikutza valley (which is a protected natural reserve), may have specific access rules or require prior registration, especially for larger groups. It's always advisable to check local regulations for the specific area you plan to visit before your hike.

Q: What are the parking options for waterfall hikes in Bajo Bidasoa?
A: Parking availability varies depending on the trailhead. Many popular starting points for waterfall hikes, especially those near villages or natural park entrances, offer designated parking areas. For example, routes starting from Endara or Eskas, which lead to prominent waterfalls, typically have parking facilities nearby. It's recommended to check the specific route details on komoot for information on parking at your chosen trailhead.

Q: Can I reach any waterfall trails by public transport?
A: While many trailheads in the more remote parts of Bajo Bidasoa are best accessed by car, some areas might be reachable via public transport, particularly those closer to larger towns like Hondarribia or Irun. The Bidasoa Greenway, for instance, is accessible at various points, and local bus services might connect to certain villages that serve as starting points for hikes. It's best to research local bus schedules for the specific trailhead you intend to visit.
tips: Short climb, mostly in shade. When you get to the San Narciso palace, the road narrows and you have to be careful.

The Aritxulegi tunnel at 440 meters above sea level represents the border crossing between the Foral Community of Navarra and the Basque Country. The surrounding landscape is very picturesque with the Endara-San Antón reservoir very close to it.
In between two classic Basque climbs lies lake Endara, good spot to have a snack!

Nice climb up a very quiet road, which runs through a leafy forest, although from time to time the landscape opens up and you can admire the views of the valley and the Endara reservoir.

The slope from Endara is 4.2 kilometers long, with an average gradient of 6.8%. Around kilometer 3 is when the hardest percentages are found, around 8 and 10%.

If you go up from Lesaka the climb is twice as long, but it doesn't really start to get steep until the second kilometer. In any case, it is not a very steep climb until the last kilometer, where there are ramps of up to 11%.
Cement track, hard, but through a beautiful forest stretch

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging racebike route, covering approximately 53.3 km with significant elevation gain, typically takes around 3 hours and 8 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is rated as 'difficult' and is best suited for cyclists with a very good fitness level. It features two notable climbs, Collado de Aritxulegi and Alto Agiña, with average gradients often exceeding 6% and some sections reaching up to 12%.

faqs.question: Where can I park my car to start this cycling route in Irún?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting and ending in Irún, you can look for public parking options within the city. Specific parking details for cyclists are not provided, but Irún offers various parking facilities where you can leave your vehicle before beginning your ride.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and road conditions can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route features mostly well-paved surfaces, making it ideal for road bikes. Both the Collado de Aritxulegi and Alto Agiña climbs are known for good road conditions and very little traffic, especially on the ascents themselves. You'll encounter tight hairpin bends on Aritxulegi and looping hairpin bends on Alto Agiña.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience pleasant scenery with a mix of dense woodland, forest, and pastures. While some sections are primarily forested, the landscape opens up at various points, offering expansive views of the valley, particularly upon reaching the pass of Aritxulegi from Oiartzun, which is within the Aiako Harria Natural Park.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: This route is accessible year-round. However, for the most comfortable experience, consider cycling during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Always check the weather forecast before you go, especially for conditions on the higher passes.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights or landmarks I'll encounter on the route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the challenging Ascent to Laza and Rifugio (Bentas), the unique Aritxulegi Tunnel (remember a light!), the serene Endara-San Anton Reservoir, and the iconic Alto de Agiña. You'll also ride along a scenic Concrete Path Through the Forest.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route or Aiako Harria Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or to access the Aiako Harria Natural Park through which parts of the route pass. However, always respect local regulations and natural park guidelines.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for racebiking on paved roads and involves significant climbs and descents. It is generally not considered suitable for cycling with dogs, especially given the challenging nature and potential for traffic, even if low.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths or trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with trails like Bidasoko Bide Berdea / Vía Verde del Bidasoa, Senda Pirenaica - E37, and Arditurriko bidegorria, among others. This offers opportunities to explore further if you wish.

faqs.question: What makes this route particularly special for cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is notable for its connection to professional cycling; Alto Agiña has been featured as a climb in the prestigious Tour of the Basque Country stage race. This, combined with well-maintained, low-traffic roads, challenging climbs, and picturesque Basque Country scenery, makes it a highly regarded ride for enthusiasts.
